content:
# Research: State of the Art in Extracting "Why" From Codebases (March 2026)

## The Four "Why" Gaps

When onboarding a repo mid-life, the hardest knowledge to extract automatically:
1. Why decisions were made (design rationale)
2. Business context driving the code
3. Known technical debt and intentional shortcuts
4. Future plans and roadmap intent

## Who Is Working on This

### 1. Lore Protocol (arxiv 2603.15566, March 2026)
- Author: Ivan Stetsenko
- Concept: "Decision Shadow" -- each commit captures a diff but discards the reasoning behind it
- Solution: Git trailers (native git feature) to serialize constraints, rejected alternatives, agent directives, verification metadata into commit messages
- Key insight: "Rejected trailers prevent agents from re-exploring dead ends"
- Limitation: Only works going FORWARD. Does not retroactively recover rationale from existing repos.
- CLI tool: `lore query` for shell-based discovery

### 2. lorehq/lore (Product, 2025-2026)
- Tagline: "Your coding agent forgets everything between sessions. Lore fixes that."
- A harness for AI coding agents: persistent memory, convention enforcement, skill capture
- Cross-platform (Claude Code, Cursor, OpenCode)
- Captures agent session knowledge as "skills" that persist
- Limitation: Agent-centric memory, not codebase archaeology. Doesn't mine existing git history for rationale.

### 3. AvraamMavridis/lore (Project, 2026)
- "High-context repository where AI agents read the minds of previous developers"
- Stores: Intent, Reasoning Trace (full chain-of-thought), Rejected Alternatives
- Cryptographically linked to file content hashes and git commits
- Limitation: Requires manual or agent-driven entry at write time. Not retroactive.

### 4. git-why (muin-company, 2025)
- AI-powered git history explainer
- Uses git blame + commit messages + diffs to explain why code exists
- Turns raw git history into human-readable narrative
- Limitation: Quality depends on commit message quality. "fix bug" yields nothing.

### 5. LLM Design Rationale Generation (arxiv 2504.20781, April 2025)
- Tested 5 LLMs generating design rationale from 100 architecture problems (50 SO posts, 25 GitHub issues, 25 GitHub discussions)
- Results: Precision 0.267-0.278, Recall 0.627-0.715, F1 0.351-0.389
- Critical finding: 64-69% of LLM-generated arguments NOT mentioned by human experts were also rated helpful
- Implication: LLMs over-generate but the novel arguments are mostly useful, not noise
- Prompting: Zero-shot, CoT, and agent strategies all performed similarly

### 6. CodeScene (Commercial, established)
- Behavioral code analysis: temporal coupling, hotspot analysis, code health scores
- Mines git history for organizational patterns (who changes what together, change frequency)
- Quantitative signals: "this file changes with that file 80% of the time" = coupling
- Limitation: Quantitative only. Detects patterns but doesn't explain rationale.

### 7. Blar (Commercial, 2025-2026)
- Codebase-aware intelligence for tech debt via PR analysis
- 4 specialized agents (General, Debugger, Optimizer, Security)
- Analyzed 10,000+ PRs
- Limitation: Forward-looking (PR review), not retroactive knowledge extraction.

### 8. Technical Debt Master (tdm, 2025)
- Local LLM-powered proactive tech debt detection
- Transforms reactive debt tracking into automated system
- Limitation: Detects debt patterns, doesn't explain why they exist.

## The Gap No One Has Filled

Each tool picks ONE signal source:
- Lore/lorehq: Agent session memory (forward only)
- git-why: Git commit messages (retroactive but low signal)
- CodeScene: Git behavioral patterns (quantitative, not qualitative)
- LLM rationale paper: LLM inference from discussion context (promising recall, low precision)
- Blar/tdm: PR/code pattern analysis (forward only)

NO tool combines all four signal sources into a unified synthesis:
1. Code structure (AST, call graph, imports) -- what corvia already has
2. Git history (commits, blame, diffs over time) -- temporal evolution
3. PR/issue context (descriptions, review comments, linked issues) -- human discussion
4. LLM inference (synthesize rationale from combined signals) -- the generation step

## Opportunity for Corvia

Corvia is uniquely positioned because:
- Already has code structure (adapter-git + tree-sitter + graph edges)
- Already has a knowledge graph to store synthesized understanding
- Already has a generation engine (RAG pipeline)
- adapter-git could be extended to mine git history (commits, diffs, blame)
- adapter-github (planned in D44) would bring PR/issue context
- The synthesis pipeline would combine all signals into knowledge entries

The retroactive "why" extraction pipeline would be:
1. Code structure analysis (existing) -- what connects to what
2. Git archaeology (new) -- how the code evolved, who changed what when
3. PR/issue mining (new, via adapter-github) -- discussions, review comments, linked issues
4. LLM synthesis (new) -- combine all three signals to generate rationale entries

This would be the first tool to do multi-signal retroactive rationale extraction at the knowledge graph level.
